card.intuition: Splitting your application into two separate models: one for 'Commands' (changing data) and one for 'Queries' (reading data).
card.analogy: A physical library: the 'Write' system is the librarians putting books on shelves. The 'Read' system is the public index catalog. They are completely different tools optimized for different speeds.
card.time_complexity: O(1) command dispatch; O(Q) query read
when_to_use: Large-scale systems where read performance needs are vastly different from write performance
When the same data needs to be presented in many different formats to users
Combining with Event Sourcing for complex business workflows
gotchas: CQRS adds massive complexity — don't use it for simple CRUD apps
It usually leads to 'Eventual Consistency' between the write DB and the read DB

steps.label: Command
steps.annotation: Command issued: CreateOrder — intent to change state, no return data.

steps.label: Command handler
steps.annotation: Handler validates command, applies business rules, updates write model.

steps.label: Event emitted
steps.annotation: Domain event published after state change.

steps.label: Query
steps.annotation: Query issued: GetOrder — read-only, no side effects.

steps.label: Read model
steps.annotation: Read model updated by event handler — optimised for query access patterns.

exercise.prompt: Identify if the action is a Command (updates state) or a Query (returns data).
hints: If it changes information, it's a Command
If it just looks at information, it's a Query
